choices: a closing formula meaning 'so be it'
steps.reveal: Amen = truly (used to introduce a solemn declaration)

hints.a Latin conjunction meaning 'but': 'Amen' is not Latin — it is a Hebrew loanword meaning 'truly'
hints.a Greek particle meaning 'therefore': 'Amen' is Hebrew, not Greek — it means 'truly' or 'verily'
hints.a closing formula meaning 'so be it': 'Amen' can close prayers, but when opening a statement (as Jesus uses it) it means 'truly'

hints.to dedicate: 'To dedicate' is 'dicare' (1st conj.) — 'dicere' (3rd conj.) means to say

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'dixi' — 'dico' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'dicam' — 'dico' is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'dicam' — 'dico' with short -o is present indicative

hints.duco, ducere, duxi, ductum: These are the parts of 'ducere' (to lead)
hints.disco, discere, didici, —: These are the parts of 'discere' (to learn)
hints.dico, dicare, dicavi, dicatum: These are the parts of 'dicare' (to dedicate) — a different 1st conjugation verb

steps.reveal: vobis = to you (dat., indirect object of 'dico')

hints.nominative: Nominative of 'vos' is 'vos' — 'vobis' is dative or ablative
hints.ablative: 'Vobis' can be ablative, but with 'dico' it is dative indirect object

steps.target: noun clause after verb of saying ('that')
choices: noun clause after verb of saying ('that')
steps.reveal: quia = that (introducing indirect discourse in Vulgate Latin)

hints.causal clause ('because'): 'Quia' classically means 'because,' but in Vulgate Latin it often means 'that' after verbs of saying
hints.relative clause: Relative clauses use 'qui/quae/quod' — 'quia' is a conjunction
hints.purpose clause: Purpose clauses use 'ut' — 'quia' here introduces reported speech

steps.reveal: nemo propheta = no prophet (nom., subject of 'acceptus est')

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'neminem prophetam' — 'nemo propheta' is nominative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'nemine propheta' — 'nemo propheta' is nominative
hints.vocative: A prophet is not being addressed — 'nemo propheta' is the subject

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'acceptus est' translates to?

hints.to approach: 'To approach' is 'accedere' — 'acceptus est' comes from 'accipere' (to receive)
hints.to take away: 'To take away' is 'auferre' — 'accipere' means to receive or accept

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'acceptus est'?
steps.reveal: acceptus est = is accepted / has been received

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'accipitur' — participle + 'est' is perfect passive
hints.pluperfect passive indicative: Pluperfect passive would be 'acceptus erat' — 'est' makes it perfect
hints.perfect active indicative: Perfect active would be 'accepit' — participle + 'est' is passive

hints.capio, capere, cepi, captum: These are the parts of 'capere' (to take) — the base verb without the ac- prefix
hints.recipio, recipere, recepi, receptum: These are the parts of 'recipere' (to take back) — different prefix
hints.incipio, incipere, incepi, inceptum: These are the parts of 'incipere' (to begin) — different compound

steps.reveal: in patria sua = in his own homeland (abl. of place where)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'patria sua' too, but 'in' + ablative marks location
hints.accusative: Accusative with 'in' would mean 'into' — ablative with 'in' means 'in'
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'patriae suae' — 'patria sua' is ablative with 'in'

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'patriae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'patriae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'patriae' ends in -ae

translation: Truly I say to you that no prophet is accepted in his own homeland
